M.S. Applied Business Analytics @ Boston University
I am a data analyst and graduate student who turns large, messy datasets into decisions. I work across the full analytics workflow, from building ETL pipelines and statistical models to benchmarking machine learning approaches and translating their outputs into clear business recommendations. My recent work spans behavioral economics, real estate valuation, and income classification, using Python and R on datasets ranging from tens of thousands to millions of records.

title: Julie Ngo Tran
text:
Featured Work



Python · scikit-learn

Boston Property Assessment
I benchmarked seven regression model families using five fold cross validation to predict residential property values. Random Forest delivered the strongest result, reaching a cross validated RMSE of USD 252,792 against a baseline of USD 500,564, roughly halving the prediction error.





R · tidyverse

Bushwick NYC Real Estate
I fit a multiple linear regression model on a transaction dataset of more than two million records, then used residual analysis to surface undervalued properties in the Bushwick neighborhood of Brooklyn.





Python · XGBoost

Census Income Classification
I built a gradient boosted classifier that reached 84 percent accuracy on a 48,842 record census dataset, handling class imbalance to keep the model honest on the minority class.





Python · launchd · Bash

Minecraft Auto Close
My first shipped automation tool, a macOS utility that closes Minecraft and CurseForge on a configurable schedule, built around scheduled process management, logging, and launchd. Read more.




Coursework and lab projects from my Boston University program are collected under the Projects menu above.

title: About Me
text:
I am Julie Ngo Tran, a graduate student in Applied Business Analytics at Boston University and a data analyst based in the New York City metropolitan area. I enjoy the part of analytics where a question becomes a dataset, a dataset becomes a model, and a model becomes a recommendation that someone can actually act on. My background spans the entire workflow, from extracting and cleaning raw data, through statistical modeling and multi model benchmarking, to interpreting results and communicating them to a non technical audience.
Most recently I led the machine learning work for the team that won first place at the Applied Business Analytics Hackathon 2026 at Boston University MET, where we predicted streaming subscription upgrade behavior across 1.17M behavioral economics records. That project is a good summary of how I like to work, combining several modeling approaches, comparing them honestly, and using SHAP analysis to explain why the model makes the predictions it does rather than treating it as a black box.

title: Spark SQL Job Data Analysis
section: Query 1: How many job postings are in the dataset?
text:
Query 1: How many job postings are in the dataset?

result_q1 = spark.sql("""
    SELECT COUNT(*) AS total_job_postings
    FROM jobs
""")

result_q1.show()

Insight: This query counts the total number of rows in the dataset, giving us the overall size of the Lightcast job postings collection. Each row represents one job posting.

title: Spark SQL Job Data Analysis
section: Query 2: Find the top 5 most common job titles
text:
Query 2: Find the top 5 most common job titles

result_q2 = spark.sql("""
    SELECT TITLE_NAME, COUNT(*) AS posting_count
    FROM jobs
    GROUP BY TITLE_NAME
    ORDER BY posting_count DESC
    LIMIT 5
""")

result_q2.show(truncate=False)

Insight: This query identifies the most in-demand roles in the dataset. The top job titles reveal which positions employers are hiring for most frequently, offering a snapshot of labor market demand.

title: Spark SQL Job Data Analysis
section: Query 3: Find the average salary for each employment type
text:
Query 3: Find the average salary for each employment type

result_q3 = spark.sql("""
    SELECT 
        EMPLOYMENT_TYPE_NAME,
        ROUND(AVG(SALARY), 2) AS avg_salary,
        COUNT(*) AS job_count
    FROM jobs
    WHERE SALARY IS NOT NULL
    GROUP BY EMPLOYMENT_TYPE_NAME
    ORDER BY avg_salary DESC
""")

result_q3.show(truncate=False)

Insight: Comparing average salaries across employment types (e.g., Full-time vs. Part-time vs. Contract) highlights compensation differences. Full-time roles typically command higher average salaries than part-time or contract positions.

title: Spark SQL Job Data Analysis
section: Query 4: What five states have the most job postings?
text:
Query 4: What five states have the most job postings?

result_q4 = spark.sql("""
    SELECT STATE_NAME, COUNT(*) AS posting_count
    FROM jobs
    WHERE STATE_NAME IS NOT NULL
    GROUP BY STATE_NAME
    ORDER BY posting_count DESC
    LIMIT 5
""")

result_q4.show(truncate=False)

Insight: This query surfaces the states with the highest concentration of job postings, indicating regional hiring activity. States like California, Texas, and New York typically dominate job posting volumes due to their large economies.

title: Spark SQL Job Data Analysis
section: Query 5: Salary range (max − min) for each job title in California
text:
Query 5: Salary range (max − min) for each job title in California

result_q5 = spark.sql("""
    SELECT 
        TITLE_NAME,
        MAX(SALARY) AS max_salary,
        MIN(SALARY) AS min_salary,
        ROUND(MAX(SALARY) - MIN(SALARY), 2) AS salary_range
    FROM jobs
    WHERE STATE_NAME = 'California'
      AND SALARY IS NOT NULL
    GROUP BY TITLE_NAME
    ORDER BY salary_range DESC
    LIMIT 10
""")

result_q5.show(truncate=False)

Insight: A wide salary range for a job title in California suggests significant variation across employers or experience levels. Roles with large ranges (like Software Engineer or Data Scientist) often have tiered compensation structures.

title: Spark SQL Job Data Analysis
section: Query 6: Top 5 industries with highest average salaries and more than 100 job postings
text:
Query 6: Top 5 industries with highest average salaries and more than 100 job postings

result_q6 = spark.sql("""
    SELECT 
        NAICS2_NAME AS industry,
        ROUND(AVG(SALARY), 2) AS avg_salary,
        COUNT(*) AS job_count
    FROM jobs
    WHERE SALARY IS NOT NULL
      AND NAICS2_NAME IS NOT NULL
    GROUP BY NAICS2_NAME
    HAVING COUNT(*) > 100
    ORDER BY avg_salary DESC
    LIMIT 5
""")

result_q6.show(truncate=False)

Insight: This query filters to industries with meaningful sample sizes (>100 postings) and ranks them by average salary. High-paying industries with many postings — such as Finance or Technology — represent the most lucrative and active sectors in the labor market.

Methodology note. Because many job postings do not state a salary, the missing values in the salary columns are filled with their dataset medians so that every row stays usable. This imputation has one important side effect that shapes the later sections: once more than half of the rows share the same filled-in value, any salary median computed within a group collapses toward that single imputed figure. For that reason the occupation analysis in Section 3 measures its median over the real, non-imputed salaries, while the experience scatter plots in Sections 4 and 5 keep the imputed average salary and therefore show a dense horizontal band of points at the level of that imputed value.

title: Assignment 03 — Big Data Visualization on Scale
section: Section 3 — Salary Analysis by ONET Occupation Type
text:
Section 3 — Salary Analysis by ONET Occupation Type
Methodology and justification. The assignment specifies ONET_NAME for the horizontal axis, but in this Lightcast extract every row carries the same ONET label of Business Intelligence Analysts, so grouping by it would reduce the chart to a single bubble that compares nothing. To follow the stated direction of comparing how salaries differ across occupation types, the analysis groups on LOT_V6_OCCUPATION_NAME instead, which resolves to the six occupations whose names and posting counts match the example table in the assignment exactly and therefore appears to be the intended level of detail. The median is taken only over real SALARY_FROM and SALARY_TO values for the reason given in the cleaning note above, and the figure is drawn as a pay against demand bubble chart so that the horizontal axis, the number of postings on a log scale, and the vertical axis, the median salary, each carry meaning rather than repeating the occupation on both the axis and the color.

# ONET_NAME is single-valued in this extract, so we group by LOT occupation
# to follow the assignment's direction (salary across occupation types).
# Median is computed on REAL salary only: SALARY_FROM / SALARY_TO are never
# imputed, so they reflect actual pay. Job_Postings = total postings (demand).
real_avg = when(
    col('SALARY_FROM').isNotNull() & (col('SALARY_FROM') > 0) &
    col('SALARY_TO').isNotNull() & (col('SALARY_TO') > 0),
    (col('SALARY_FROM') + col('SALARY_TO')) / 2
)

df3 = (
    df.filter(col('LOT_V6_OCCUPATION_NAME').isNotNull())
    .withColumn('REAL_AVG', real_avg)
    .groupBy('LOT_V6_OCCUPATION_NAME')
    .agg(
        F.percentile_approx('REAL_AVG', 0.5).alias('Median_Salary'),
        F.count('*').alias('Job_Postings')
    )
    .orderBy(F.desc('Job_Postings'))
)

pdf3 = df3.toPandas()
pdf3.columns = ['Occupation_Name', 'Median_Salary', 'Job_Postings']
print(pdf3)

# Pay-vs-demand bubble chart: x = demand (postings, log), y = pay (median),
# size = postings, each bubble labelled with its occupation.
fig3 = px.scatter(
    pdf3,
    x='Job_Postings',
    y='Median_Salary',
    size='Job_Postings',
    color='Occupation_Name',
    text='Occupation_Name',
    log_x=True,
    title='Occupation Pay vs. Demand (Bubble Size = Number of Postings)',
    labels={
        'Job_Postings': 'Number of Job Postings (log scale)',
        'Median_Salary': 'Median Salary ($)',
        'Occupation_Name': 'Occupation'
    },
    size_max=70
)
fig3.update_traces(textposition='top center', textfont=dict(size=11))
fig3.update_layout(height=640, showlegend=False, margin=dict(t=80, r=60, b=70))
fig3.show()

title: Assignment 03 — Big Data Visualization on Scale
text:
Section 4 — Salary by Education Level
Methodology and justification. Each posting lists every level of education the employer will accept rather than a single requirement, so a posting is assigned to the highest qualification it names, running from doctorate down through master, then bachelor, and finally associate or lower. The doctorate level is written in this dataset as Ph.D. or professional degree, so the grouping searches for the text Ph.D. or professional; without this rule the PhD group would be empty because the simpler spellings never appear. The associate or lower group then absorbs the high school, GED, associate, and no education listed postings, as the assignment intends.

# Select only needed columns before toPandas
df4 = df.filter(
    col('MAX_YEARS_EXPERIENCE').isNotNull() &
    col('Average_Salary').isNotNull() &
    col('EDUCATION_LEVELS_NAME').isNotNull() &
    col('LOT_V6_SPECIALIZED_OCCUPATION_NAME').isNotNull()
).select(
    col('MAX_YEARS_EXPERIENCE').alias('MAX_EXPERIENCE'),
    col('Average_Salary').alias('AVERAGE_SALARY'),
    col('EDUCATION_LEVELS_NAME'),
    col('LOT_V6_SPECIALIZED_OCCUPATION_NAME').alias('OCCUPATION_NAME')
)

# Postings list every degree accepted, so we assign each to its HIGHEST level.
# Doctorates appear as "Ph.D. or professional degree".
df4 = df4.withColumn(
    'EDU_GROUP',
    when(
        col('EDUCATION_LEVELS_NAME').contains('Ph.D.') |
        col('EDUCATION_LEVELS_NAME').contains('professional'),
        'PhD'
    )
    .when(col('EDUCATION_LEVELS_NAME').contains('Master'), 'Master')
    .when(col('EDUCATION_LEVELS_NAME').contains('Bachelor'), 'Bachelor')
    .otherwise('Associate or Lower')
)

pdf4 = df4.select('MAX_EXPERIENCE','AVERAGE_SALARY','EDU_GROUP','OCCUPATION_NAME').toPandas()
print(pdf4['EDU_GROUP'].value_counts())
print(pdf4.head())

edu_groups = ['Associate or Lower', 'Bachelor', 'Master', 'PhD']
edu_insights = {
    'Associate or Lower': 'This group covers High school, GED, Associate, and postings that list no education '
                          'requirement, and its salaries span the widest range. Many points fall on the imputed '
                          'median band near USD 109K (where listings omitted pay), but real disclosed salaries reach '
                          'well into six figures, showing analyst roles remain accessible without a degree.',
    'Bachelor':           'Bachelor-as-highest-requirement is the most common group and is dominated by Data / Data '
                          'Mining and Business Intelligence Analyst roles. Salaries rise gently with experience and '
                          'mostly sit in the USD 90K to USD 130K band, reflecting the mainstream analyst labor market.',
    'Master':             'Master-level postings carry a higher average salary than bachelor roles and lean toward '
                          'Business Intelligence and Computer Systems Engineering occupations. The distribution is '
                          'tighter at the upper end, indicating these listings target more specialized positions.',
    'PhD':                'Ph.D. / professional-degree postings are the smallest group but reach the highest salary '
                          'ceilings, especially Computer Systems Engineer / Architect roles. Points are sparse and '
                          'experience matters less here, suggesting the credential itself drives the premium.'
}

for group in edu_groups:
    subset = pdf4[pdf4['EDU_GROUP'] == group].copy()
    if subset.empty:
        print(f'No rows for {group}; skipping.')
        continue
    subset['JITTER_X'] = subset['MAX_EXPERIENCE'] + np.random.uniform(-0.3, 0.3, len(subset))

    fig = px.scatter(
        subset,
        x='JITTER_X',
        y='AVERAGE_SALARY',
        color='OCCUPATION_NAME',
        title=f'Salary by Education Level: {group}',
        labels={'JITTER_X': 'Max Years Experience (jittered)', 'AVERAGE_SALARY': 'Average Salary ($)'},
        opacity=0.7,
        height=500
    )
    fig.update_layout(
        height=680,
        margin=dict(t=70, b=200),
        xaxis_title_standoff=12,
        legend=dict(orientation='h', yanchor='top', y=-0.22,
                    x=0.5, xanchor='center', title_text='')
    )
    fig.show()
    display(Markdown(f"**Insight ({group}):** {edu_insights[group]}"))

Many points fall on the imputed median band near USD 109K (where listings omitted pay), but real disclosed salaries reach well into six figures, showing analyst roles remain accessible without a degree.


                                                    


Insight (Bachelor): Bachelor-as-highest-requirement is the most common group and is dominated by Data / Data Mining and Business Intelligence Analyst roles. Salaries rise gently with experience and mostly sit in the USD 90K to USD 130K band, reflecting the mainstream analyst labor market.


                                                    


Insight (Master): Master-level postings carry a higher average salary than bachelor roles and lean toward Business Intelligence and Computer Systems Engineering occupations. The distribution is tighter at the upper end, indicating these listings target more specialized positions.


                                                    


Insight (PhD): Ph.D. / professional-degree postings are the smallest group but reach the highest salary ceilings, especially Computer Systems Engineer / Architect roles. Points are sparse and experience matters less here, suggesting the credential itself drives the premium.

title: Module 5 Assignment: Machine Learning on Scale
text:
4.1 Linear Regression Summary
The fitted model now reports standard errors, t statistics, and p values for every coefficient, which confirms that dropping the redundant maximum experience column restored a full rank feature matrix. Each estimate is read as the change in predicted annual salary in United States dollars for a one unit change in that feature while the others are held constant, and a feature is treated as statistically significant when its p value falls below the conventional five percent threshold. Minimum years of experience is by far the most important predictor, with each additional required year associated with about seven thousand four hundred United States dollars of additional salary at a t statistic above seventy four and a p value below one in ten thousand, so the effect is both large and highly reliable. The education requirements move salary in sensible ways relative to the doctoral and professional degree baseline, which is the smallest group in the data and so is best read as a broad reference rather than a precise one, in that requiring only a high school credential or an associate degree is associated with roughly forty five thousand United States dollars less while requiring a master’s degree is associated with about thirty thousand dollars more, and all three of these effects are statistically significant. By contrast the posting duration, the remote indicator, the staffing indicator, and the employment type are all statistically insignificant, which tells us that once experience and education are accounted for these factors add little explanatory power. The model explains about thirty seven percent of the variation in salary on the held out test data with a typical prediction error of roughly thirty three thousand seven hundred United States dollars, a moderate fit that reflects how much of advertised pay depends on role, company, and skills that these few structured columns do not capture, and these values form the baseline that the polynomial and random forest models are measured against.

title: Module 5 Assignment: Machine Learning on Scale
text:
5.1 Polynomial Regression Summary
The polynomial model adds the squared minimum experience term so that the marginal effect of an extra year of required experience is allowed to change as experience grows. The estimates show exactly the shape we expected, with the linear experience term large and positive at about eleven thousand six hundred United States dollars per year and the squared term negative at about minus three hundred and twenty dollars, and the squared term is highly significant with a t statistic near minus thirteen and a p value below one in ten thousand. A positive linear term paired with a significant negative squared term means salary rises steeply with early experience but each additional year adds less, the classic pattern of diminishing returns at senior levels. Setting the marginal effect to zero shows that predicted salary stops rising at roughly eighteen years of required experience and then gently turns down, which is a sensible ceiling for advertised pay. The education and other coefficients keep the same signs and significance as in the linear model, so adding the curve does not disturb the rest of the story. On the held out test data the polynomial model reaches an R squared of about thirty eight percent with a typical error near thirty three thousand four hundred United States dollars, a small but genuine improvement over the plain linear model, and because the squared term is strongly significant the added complexity is justified rather than gratuitous. These values are carried into the final comparison section where all three models are judged together.

title: Module 5 Assignment: Machine Learning on Scale
text:
Hyperparameter Selection
The assignment asks us to choose a number of trees between one hundred and five hundred and a maximum depth between four and ten. These are hyperparameters, which means they are settings we choose before training rather than values the model estimates from the data, so there is no formula that returns the best choice in advance and we instead search for good values and record the evidence. We document this search even though the assignment only requires us to pick numbers, because the two settings behave very differently and seeing that difference is what justifies the final choice. The first sweep varies the number of trees while holding depth fixed, and the second varies depth while holding the number of trees fixed and also tracks the gap between training and testing accuracy as an early warning sign of overfitting.

from pyspark.ml.regression import RandomForestRegressor

train_data.cache()
test_data.cache()
rmse_eval = RegressionEvaluator(labelCol="SALARY", predictionCol="prediction", metricName="rmse")
r2_eval = RegressionEvaluator(labelCol="SALARY", predictionCol="prediction", metricName="r2")
mae_eval = RegressionEvaluator(labelCol="SALARY", predictionCol="prediction", metricName="mae")

# Sweep 1: vary numTrees with maxDepth held at 5.
tree_rows = []
for n in [200, 300, 400, 500]:
    m = RandomForestRegressor(featuresCol="features", labelCol="SALARY",
                              numTrees=n, maxDepth=5, seed=42).fit(train_data)
    pred = m.transform(test_data)
    tree_rows.append((n, round(r2_eval.evaluate(pred), 4), round(rmse_eval.evaluate(pred), 2)))

tree_sweep = pd.DataFrame(tree_rows, columns=["numTrees", "Test R2", "Test RMSE"])

                              numTrees=200, maxDepth=d, seed=42).fit(train_data)

The first sweep shows that test accuracy barely moves as the number of trees grows from two hundred to five hundred, improving the root mean squared error by less than a tenth of a percent while the fitting time roughly doubles. This is the expected behaviour of a random forest, where adding trees steadies the averaged prediction until it reaches a plateau and then mostly adds cost, so we settle on two hundred trees as the point where the accuracy is already realised. The second sweep shows that deeper trees keep improving test accuracy across the whole allowed range, but the gap between training and testing accuracy widens steadily from about one to about five percentage points, which is the early signature of overfitting where the model begins to fit the training data better than the unseen data. The largest depth gives the best test accuracy but also the widest gap, so we choose a maximum depth of eight as a balanced setting that keeps most of the accuracy while holding the overfitting gap to a moderate level. The final model below therefore uses two hundred trees and a maximum depth of eight.

# Final random forest with the selected hyperparameters.
rf = RandomForestRegressor(featuresCol="features", labelCol="SALARY",
                           numTrees=200, maxDepth=8, seed=42)
rf_model = rf.fit(train_data)
rf_pred = rf_model.transform(test_data)

rf_r2 = r2_eval.evaluate(rf_pred)
rf_rmse = rmse_eval.evaluate(rf_pred)
rf_mae = mae_eval.evaluate(rf_pred)

print("Test R2:", round(rf_r2, 4))
print("Test RMSE:", round(rf_rmse, 2))
print("Test MAE:", round(rf_mae, 2))

The minimum education level contributes the next largest share, while the posting duration and the remote indicator add only small amounts and the employment type and staffing indicators matter least. This agreement between the regression models and a very different tree based model gives extra confidence that experience is the main driver of pay in this data rather than an artefact of one modelling choice.

title: Module 5 Assignment: Machine Learning on Scale
section: 7.1 Calculating Log-Likelihood and BIC for PySpark Models
text:
7.1 Calculating Log-Likelihood and BIC for PySpark Models
The Akaike information criterion is reported directly by the generalized linear model summary, but the Bayesian information criterion is not, so we build it from the model deviance. The log-likelihood, which measures how probable the observed salaries are under the fitted model, is recovered as negative one half times the quantity n times the natural log of two pi, plus n times the natural log of the dispersion, plus the deviance divided by the dispersion, where n is the number of training observations, the dispersion is the model variance estimate, and the deviance measures the unexplained variation. The Bayesian information criterion is then k times the natural log of n minus two times the log-likelihood, where k is the number of estimated parameters. Because the Bayesian criterion multiplies the parameter count by the natural log of the sample size rather than by two, it penalises extra parameters more heavily than the Akaike criterion does, so it favours simpler models more strongly. For both criteria a lower value indicates a better balance of fit and simplicity.

import math

def glm_log_likelihood(summary, n_obs):
    disp = summary.dispersion
    dev = summary.deviance
    return -0.5 * (n_obs * math.log(2 * math.pi) + n_obs * math.log(disp) + dev / disp)

def glm_bic(summary, n_obs, k_params):
    log_lik = glm_log_likelihood(summary, n_obs)
    return k_params * math.log(n_obs) - 2 * log_lik

n_obs = train_data.count()
lr_k = len(lr_model.coefficients) + 1
poly_k = len(poly_model.coefficients) + 1
lr_bic = glm_bic(train_sum, n_obs, lr_k)
poly_bic = glm_bic(poly_train_sum, n_obs, poly_k)

metrics = pd.DataFrame({
    "Model": ["GLR (Linear)", "Polynomial", "Random Forest"],
    "Test RMSE": [round(lr_rmse, 2), round(poly_rmse, 2), round(rf_rmse, 2)],
    "Test R2": [round(lr_r2, 4), round(poly_r2, 4), round(rf_r2, 4)],
    "AIC": [f"{train_sum.aic:,.2f}", f"{poly_train_sum.aic:,.2f}", "N/A"],
    "BIC": [f"{lr_bic:,.2f}", f"{poly_bic:,.2f}", "N/A"],
})

title: Module 5 Assignment: Machine Learning on Scale
text:
7.2 Evaluation Metrics
The comparison table and the diagnostic plots below are read together. In the plots each point is one test posting, with its actual salary on the horizontal axis and the model’s predicted salary on the vertical axis, and the red dashed line marks perfect prediction, so a model whose points hug that line more tightly is the more accurate one.

import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import seaborn as sns

model_order = ["GLR", "Polynomial", "Random Forest"]
fig, axes = plt.subplots(2, 2, figsize=(11, 9))
axes = axes.flatten()
for ax, name in zip(axes, model_order):
    sub = pred_long[pred_long["Model"] == name]
    sns.scatterplot(data=sub, x="SALARY", y="Predicted", ax=ax, alpha=0.3, s=15)
    lo = min(sub["SALARY"].min(), sub["Predicted"].min())
    hi = max(sub["SALARY"].max(), sub["Predicted"].max())
    ax.plot([lo, hi], [lo, hi], "r--", linewidth=1)
    ax.set_title(f"{name}: Actual vs Predicted")
    ax.set_xlabel("Actual SALARY (USD)")
    ax.set_ylabel("Predicted SALARY (USD)")
axes[3].axis("off")
plt.tight_layout()
plt.savefig("_output/model_comparison.png", dpi=150)
plt.show()








The metrics and the plots agree on the ranking. The random forest gives the lowest root mean squared error and the highest test R squared of the three models, so it is the most accurate predictor of advertised salary on unseen postings, and its scatter of points sits closest to the perfect prediction line. Between the two generalized linear models the polynomial model has both the lower Akaike and the lower Bayesian information criterion, which confirms that the squared experience term earns its place rather than just adding complexity, and it also edges out the plain linear model on test error. The information criteria cannot rank the random forest because it has no likelihood, so the choice of best model depends on the goal. If the goal is the most accurate salary prediction then the random forest is the clear choice, while if the goal is an interpretable model with coefficients, significance tests, and information criteria for inference, then the polynomial generalized linear model is the preferred option. A practical workflow would use the polynomial model to explain which factors move salary and by how much, and the random forest to generate the most accurate point predictions.

    color='EMPLOYMENT_TYPE_NAME'
)

fig2.write_image('_output/figure2.svg')

text:
9. Career Pathway Trends (Sankey Diagram)

df9 = df.filter(F.col('NAICS2_NAME').isNotNull() & F.col('REMOTE_TYPE_NAME').isNotNull())
df9 = df9.withColumn('REMOTE_TYPE_NAME',
    F.when(F.col('REMOTE_TYPE_NAME') == '[None]', 'Not Specified')
     .otherwise(F.col('REMOTE_TYPE_NAME'))
)
df9_agg = df9.groupBy('NAICS2_NAME', 'REMOTE_TYPE_NAME').count() \
             .orderBy(F.col('count').desc()).limit(40)
pdf9 = df9_agg.toPandas()

sources = pdf9['NAICS2_NAME'].tolist()
targets = pdf9['REMOTE_TYPE_NAME'].tolist()
all_nodes = list(dict.fromkeys(sources + targets))
node_idx = {n: i for i, n in enumerate(all_nodes)}

fig9 = go.Figure(go.Sankey(
    node=dict(
        pad=15,
        thickness=20,
        line=dict(color='#1a1a2e', width=0.5),
        label=all_nodes,
        color='#0f3460'
    ),
    link=dict(
        source=[node_idx[s] for s in sources],
        target=[node_idx[t] for t in targets],
        value=pdf9['count'].tolist(),
        color='rgba(233,69,96,0.3)'
    )
))
fig9.update_layout(
    title='Career Pathway Trends: Industry → Work Arrangement',
    font=dict(family='Arial, sans-serif', size=12, color='#16213e'),
    paper_bgcolor='#f8f9fa'
)
fig9.write_image('_output/figure9.svg')
fig9.show()

Industries with thicker remote flow links signal greater employer flexibility, which can guide job seekers in targeting sectors aligned with their preferred work arrangements.

import platform
import psutil
import subprocess

print("Operating System:", platform.system(), platform.release())
print("Python Version:", platform.python_version())
print("Machine:", platform.machine())

# EC2 Linux doesn't expose processor via platform.processor()
# Use lscpu instead
try:
    processor = subprocess.check_output("lscpu | grep 'Model name'", shell=True).decode().strip()
    processor = processor.split(":")[1].strip()
except:
    processor = "Unable to retrieve"
print("Processor:", processor)

mem = psutil.virtual_memory()
print("Total Memory (MB):", round(mem.total / (1024**2), 2))
print("Available Memory (MB):", round(mem.available / (1024**2), 2))

from pyspark.sql import SparkSession

# Start a Spark session
spark = SparkSession.builder.config("spark.driver.host", "localhost").appName("JobPostingsAnalysis").getOrCreate()

# Load the CSV file into a Spark DataFrame
df = spark.read.option("header", "true").option("inferSchema", "true").option(
    "multiLine", "true").option("escape", "\"").csv("data/lightcast_job_postings.csv")

df.createOrReplaceTempView("job_postings")

title: Module 2 Assignment: Spark SQL and DataFrames
section: 2 Step 2: Create Relational Tables in Spark SQL
text:
2 Step 2: Create Relational Tables in Spark SQL

2.1 Industries Table

from pyspark.sql.functions import col, monotonically_increasing_id

industries_df = df.select(
    col("NAICS_2022_6"),
    col("NAICS_2022_6_NAME"),
    col("SOC_5"),
    col("SOC_5_NAME"),
    col("LOT_SPECIALIZED_OCCUPATION_NAME"),
    col("LOT_OCCUPATION_GROUP")
).distinct().withColumn("industry_id", monotonically_increasing_id())

industries_df = industries_df.select(
    "industry_id",
    "NAICS_2022_6",
    "NAICS_2022_6_NAME",
    "SOC_5",
    "SOC_5_NAME",
    "LOT_SPECIALIZED_OCCUPATION_NAME",
    "LOT_OCCUPATION_GROUP"
)

# Save as CSV
industries_df.write.mode("overwrite").csv("output/industries.csv", header=True)

# Register as SQL view
industries_df.createOrReplaceTempView("industries")
industries_df.show(5, truncate=False)

# Pandas conversion for display
industries_df.toPandas()



2.2 Companies Table

companies_df = df.select(
    col("COMPANY"),
    col("COMPANY_NAME"),
    col("COMPANY_RAW"),
    col("COMPANY_IS_STAFFING")
).distinct().withColumn("company_id", monotonically_increasing_id())

companies_df = companies_df.select(
    "company_id",
    "COMPANY",
    "COMPANY_NAME",
    "COMPANY_RAW",
    "COMPANY_IS_STAFFING"
)

# Save as CSV
companies_df.write.mode("overwrite").csv("output/companies.csv", header=True)

# Register as SQL view
companies_df.createOrReplaceTempView("companies")
companies_df.show(5, truncate=False)

# Pandas conversion for display
companies_df.toPandas()



2.3 Locations Table

locations_df = df.select(
    col("LOCATION"),
    col("CITY_NAME"),
    col("STATE_NAME"),
    col("COUNTY_NAME"),
    col("MSA"),
    col("MSA_NAME")
).distinct().withColumn("location_id", monotonically_increasing_id())

locations_df = locations_df.select(
    "location_id",
    "LOCATION",
    "CITY_NAME",
    "STATE_NAME",
    "COUNTY_NAME",
    "MSA",
    "MSA_NAME"
)

# Save as CSV
locations_df.write.mode("overwrite").csv("output/locations.csv", header=True)

# Register as SQL view
locations_df.createOrReplaceTempView("locations")
locations_df.show(5, truncate=False)

# Pandas conversion for display
locations_df.toPandas()



2.4 Job Postings Table

df_with_id = df.withColumnRenamed("COMPANY", "jp_COMPANY") \
               .withColumnRenamed("COMPANY_NAME", "jp_COMPANY_NAME") \
               .withColumnRenamed("NAICS_2022_6", "jp_NAICS_2022_6") \
               .withColumnRenamed("SOC_5", "jp_SOC_5") \
               .withColumnRenamed("CITY_NAME", "jp_CITY_NAME") \
               .withColumnRenamed("STATE_NAME", "jp_STATE_NAME")

job_postings_df = df_with_id \
.join(companies_df, df_with_id["jp_COMPANY"] == companies_df["COMPANY"], "left") \
.join(industries_df,
    (df_with_id["jp_NAICS_2022_6"] == industries_df["NAICS_2022_6"]) &
    (df_with_id["jp_SOC_5"] == industries_df["SOC_5"]),
    "left") \
.join(locations_df,
    (df_with_id["jp_CITY_NAME"] == locations_df["CITY_NAME"]) &
    (df_with_id["jp_STATE_NAME"] == locations_df["STATE_NAME"]),
    "left") \
.select(
    df_with_id["ID"],
    df_with_id["TITLE_CLEAN"],
    companies_df["company_id"],
    industries_df["industry_id"],
    df_with_id["EMPLOYMENT_TYPE_NAME"],
    df_with_id["REMOTE_TYPE_NAME"],
    df_with_id["BODY"],
    df_with_id["MIN_YEARS_EXPERIENCE"],
    df_with_id["MAX_YEARS_EXPERIENCE"],
    df_with_id["SALARY"],
    df_with_id["SALARY_FROM"],
    df_with_id["SALARY_TO"],
    locations_df["location_id"],
    df_with_id["POSTED"],
    df_with_id["EXPIRED"],
    df_with_id["DURATION"]
)

# Register as SQL view
job_postings_df.createOrReplaceTempView("job_postings")
job_postings_df.show(5, truncate=False)

title: Module 2 Assignment: Spark SQL and DataFrames
section: 3 Step 3: Run SQL Queries in Spark
text:
3 Step 3: Run SQL Queries in Spark

3.1 Example Query: Top 5 Most Posted Job Titles

top5 = spark.sql("""
    SELECT TITLE_CLEAN, COUNT(*) AS job_count
    FROM job_postings
    GROUP BY TITLE_CLEAN
    ORDER BY job_count DESC
    LIMIT 5
""")
top5.show(truncate=False)
top5.toPandas()



3.2 Query 1: Average Salary by Tech Industry

Note on Query 1 Approach: The Lightcast dataset stores salary data as pre-aggregated industry-level values, meaning all occupations within the same NAICS code share identical salary figures. Therefore, instead of grouping by specialized occupation as instructed, we group by NAICS industry code across five major tech sectors (518210, 541511, 541512, 541519, 334111) to produce meaningful salary variation. This reflects a data limitation of Lightcast’s pre-summarized compensation model rather than an analytical choice.


query1 = spark.sql("""
    SELECT 
        i.NAICS_2022_6_NAME AS industry_name,
        ROUND(AVG((j.SALARY_FROM + j.SALARY_TO) / 2), 2) AS avg_salary,
        COUNT(DISTINCT j.ID) AS job_count
    FROM job_postings j
    JOIN industries i ON j.industry_id = i.industry_id
    WHERE i.NAICS_2022_6 IN (518210, 541511, 541512, 541519, 334111)
    AND j.SALARY_FROM IS NOT NULL
    AND j.SALARY_TO IS NOT NULL
    AND j.SALARY_FROM > 0
    AND j.SALARY_TO > 0
    GROUP BY i.NAICS_2022_6_NAME
    ORDER BY avg_salary DESC
""")

query1.show(truncate=False)
query1_pd = query1.toPandas()
query1_pd


import plotly.express as px
import textwrap

query1_pd["industry_name"] = query1_pd["industry_name"].apply(
    lambda x: "<br>".join(textwrap.wrap(x, width=40))
)

fig = px.bar(query1_pd,
    x="avg_salary",
    y="industry_name",
    color="industry_name",
    orientation="h",
    title="Average Salary by Tech Industry (NAICS 2022)",
    labels={"avg_salary": "Average Salary (USD)", "industry_name": "Industry"},
    color_discrete_sequence=px.colors.qualitative.Set2)

fig.update_layout(
    showlegend=False,
    margin=dict(l=350, r=100, t=80, b=50),
    yaxis=dict(tickfont=dict(size=11)),
    xaxis=dict(range=[0, 200000], tickformat="$,.0f"),
    height=400
)

fig.update_traces(
    texttemplate="%{x:$,.0f}",
    textposition="outside"
)

fig.show()

Insight: Electronic Computer Manufacturing ($167,149) offers the highest average salary among tech industries, significantly higher than Custom Computer Programming Services ($116,847). This suggests hardware manufacturing roles command premium compensation compared to software services, likely due to specialized skills and lower job volume.


3.3 Query 2: Top Companies with Most Remote Jobs in California, Washington and New York

query2 = spark.sql("""
    SELECT 
        c.COMPANY_NAME AS company_name,
        l.STATE_NAME AS state,
        COUNT(DISTINCT j.ID) AS remote_jobs
    FROM job_postings j
    JOIN companies c ON j.company_id = c.company_id
    JOIN locations l ON j.location_id = l.location_id
    WHERE j.REMOTE_TYPE_NAME = 'Remote'
    AND l.STATE_NAME IN ('California', 'Washington', 'New York')
    AND c.COMPANY_NAME != 'Unclassified'
    GROUP BY c.COMPANY_NAME, l.STATE_NAME
    ORDER BY remote_jobs DESC
    LIMIT 15
""")

query2.show(truncate=False)
query2_pd = query2.toPandas()
query2_pd


query2_pd_sorted = query2_pd.sort_values(["company_name", "state"])

fig = px.bar(query2_pd_sorted,
    x="remote_jobs",
    y="company_name",
    color="state",
    barmode="group",
    orientation="h",
    title="Top Companies with Remote Jobs in California, Washington and New York<br>(June - September 2024)",
    labels={"company_name": "Company", "remote_jobs": "Remote Job Postings", "state": "State"},
    color_discrete_sequence=px.colors.qualitative.Set2)

fig.update_layout(
    margin=dict(l=300, r=100, t=100, b=50),
    height=550,
    yaxis=dict(tickfont=dict(size=11))
)

fig.show()

Insight: Kaiser Permanente leads remote job postings in California with 33 postings, followed by University of California with 23. Washington state shows strong presence from BCforward and Providence, while New York is represented by The Judge Group. Healthcare and staffing companies dominate remote hiring across all three states.


3.4 Query 3: Monthly Job Posting Trends in California, Washington and New York

query3 = spark.sql("""
    SELECT 
        YEAR(to_date(j.POSTED, 'M/d/yyyy')) AS year,
        MONTH(to_date(j.POSTED, 'M/d/yyyy')) AS month,
        l.STATE_NAME AS state,
        COUNT(DISTINCT j.ID) AS job_count
    FROM job_postings j
    JOIN locations l ON j.location_id = l.location_id
    WHERE l.STATE_NAME IN ('California', 'Washington', 'New York')
    AND to_date(j.POSTED, 'M/d/yyyy') IS NOT NULL
    GROUP BY year, month, l.STATE_NAME
    ORDER BY year ASC, month ASC
""")

query3.show(truncate=False)
query3_pd = query3.toPandas()
query3_pd


query3_pd["month_year"] = query3_pd["month"].astype(str) + "/" + query3_pd["year"].astype(str)

fig = px.line(query3_pd,
    x="month_year",
    y="job_count",
    color="state",
    title="Monthly Job Posting Trends in California, Washington and New York<br>(June - September 2024)",
    labels={"month_year": "Month/Year", "job_count": "Job Postings", "state": "State"},
    markers=True,
    color_discrete_sequence=px.colors.qualitative.Set2)

fig.update_layout(
    margin=dict(l=50, r=50, t=100, b=50),
    height=450
)

fig.show()

Insight: California consistently dominates job postings across all months, peaking in June 2024. Both California and Washington show a dip in August before recovering slightly in September. New York shows steady but lower posting volume compared to California throughout the period.


3.5 Query 4: Salary Comparisons Across Major US Cities

query4 = spark.sql("""
    SELECT 
        CASE l.MSA
            WHEN 14460 THEN 'Boston'
            WHEN 47900 THEN 'Washington DC'
            WHEN 35620 THEN 'New York'
            WHEN 41860 THEN 'San Francisco'
            WHEN 42660 THEN 'Seattle'
            WHEN 31080 THEN 'Los Angeles'
            WHEN 19100 THEN 'Dallas'
            WHEN 26420 THEN 'Houston'
            WHEN 12420 THEN 'Austin'
            WHEN 34980 THEN 'Nashville'
            WHEN 28140 THEN 'Kansas City'
            WHEN 19740 THEN 'Denver'
        END AS city,
        ROUND(AVG(j.SALARY), 2) AS avg_salary,
        COUNT(DISTINCT j.ID) AS job_count
    FROM job_postings j
    JOIN locations l ON j.location_id = l.location_id
    WHERE j.SALARY IS NOT NULL
    AND j.SALARY > 0
    AND l.MSA IN (14460, 47900, 35620, 41860, 42660, 31080, 19100, 26420, 12420, 34980, 28140, 19740)
    GROUP BY l.MSA
    ORDER BY avg_salary DESC
""")

query4.show(truncate=False)
query4_pd = query4.toPandas()
query4_pd


fig = px.bar(query4_pd,
    x="city",
    y="avg_salary",
    color="city",
    title="Average Salary Across Major US Cities",
    labels={"city": "City", "avg_salary": "Average Salary (USD)"},
    color_discrete_sequence=px.colors.qualitative.Set2)

fig.update_layout(
    showlegend=False,
    xaxis_tickangle=-45,
    yaxis=dict(tickformat="$,.0f"),
    margin=dict(l=50, r=50, t=80, b=150),
    height=500
)

fig.update_traces(
    texttemplate="%{y:$,.0f}",
    textposition="outside"
)

fig.show()

Insight: San Francisco leads with the highest average salary among major US metro areas, followed by Seattle and Boston. Southern cities like Nashville, Houston and Dallas show comparatively lower average salaries, reflecting regional cost-of-living differences and industry concentration patterns.

title: Minecraft Auto Close
text:
Minecraft Auto Close was my first real automation project. I wanted to build something practical that would run on its own without me thinking about it, so I built a macOS tool that automatically closes Minecraft and CurseForge on a configurable schedule. I used AI-assisted coding throughout the process, from designing the architecture to debugging edge cases. The result is a lightweight system that has been running reliably on my Mac every day.
More importantly, this project became a turning point. The pattern I developed here, scheduled process management, configuration files, logging, and macOS launchd integration, became a template I have since applied to automate other apps and workflows. It taught me how to combine Python and Bash to deliver real, working tools, not just scripts that run once in a terminal.

More importantly, this project became a turning point. The pattern I developed here, scheduled process management, configuration files, logging, and macOS launchd integration, became a template I have since applied to automate other apps and workflows. It taught me how to combine Python and Bash to deliver real, working tools, not just scripts that run once in a terminal.

title: Minecraft Auto Close
text:
How It Works
The system has three layers. First, macOS’s built-in launchd scheduler triggers a Bash script on a daily schedule. When the script runs, it checks whether Minecraft or CurseForge is currently open. If either app is running and the current time falls within the configured shutdown window, the script sends a native macOS notification as a warning, waits briefly, then force-closes the applications.
The script is designed to be smart about edge cases. It prevents duplicate shutdowns on the same day, handles timezone-aware scheduling, and logs every action for debugging. A separate Python CLI tool lets me pause and resume the auto-close without uninstalling anything, and can sync schedule changes to the launchd agent on the fly.

title: Minecraft Auto Close
text:
Technical Stack
Bash powers the core shutdown logic, with process detection via pgrep and force-kill via pkill. Python 3 provides the CLI management tool for pause, resume, and schedule sync. macOS launchd handles native job scheduling, replacing cron on macOS, and osascript delivers native macOS notifications with sound alerts.

title: Minecraft Auto Close
text:
Key Features
The shutdown hour, minute, and timezone are all set in a single config file. Pause and resume let me temporarily disable auto-close without touching any system files, then re-enable with one command. Every action is timestamped and written to ~/Library/Logs/ for easy debugging. Duplicate prevention means the script tracks whether it has already run today, so it never kills apps twice. Live schedule sync lets me change the shutdown time in the config file and run one command to update the launchd agent automatically.

title: Minecraft Auto Close
text:
What I Learned
This project was my introduction to thinking about software as something that runs unattended. I learned how macOS manages background processes, how to write Bash scripts that handle real-world edge cases, and how to use Python as a control layer on top of shell automation. Working with AI-assisted coding tools on this project showed me how to move from idea to shipped tool quickly, and gave me confidence to apply the same approach to other automation challenges.
